ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Min Jiang Kueh exists; mapped to the nearest parent class 'pancake food product' (FOODON_03315091) as a defensible approximate anchor. A more specific term for this Singaporean thick pancake with red bean filling is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Pancakes and similar products) is the closest defensible category for Min Jiang Kueh. No Singapore-specific thick pancake entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Min Jiang Kueh or equivalent Singaporean pancake snack as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Min Jiang Kueh (red bean) weighs approximately 100 g and is typically consumed as a single snack or breakfast item.

nutrient_source.title: Min Jiang Kueh (Red Bean) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Min Jiang Kueh (Red Bean), food code F11090037. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this entry. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for a comparable filled pancake (USDA SR Legacy #18263) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pancakes and Wheat-Based Filled Snacks — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on plain pancake (GI ~67) adjusted for red bean filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Min Jiang Kueh (Red Bean) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 based on the closest analogous entry: plain wheat-flour pancake (GI ~67, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward marginally for the red bean filling which contributes dietary fibre (2.6 g/100g) and slows glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 40.39) / 100 ≈ 26.3 for a 100 g serving. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 212 mg/100g combined with high refined-carbohydrate and sugar content (12.59 g/100g) promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a piece; the combination of 212 mg sodium per 100 g and 12.59 g sugar can promote insulin-mediated sodium retention, and the absence of meaningful potassium or fibre offset provides no compensatory benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (40.39 g/100g) with moderate sugar (12.59 g/100g) and low dietary fibre (2.6 g/100g) producing a high estimated glycemic load of 26.3 per 100 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat Min Jiang Kueh (Red Bean) as an occasional food only; the estimated glycemic load of 26.3 per 100 g serving is high, and the refined wheat flour base causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation — if consumed, pair with a protein or non-starchy vegetable source to blunt the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (12.59 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate base contributing to dyslipidaemia risk; low total fat (2.91 g/100g) is a mitigating factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the low total fat (2.91 g/100g) and low saturated fat (~0.7 g/100g) are favourable, but the high refined-carbohydrate and sugar content can elevate triglycerides and contribute to dyslipidaemia; limit to one small piece and avoid frequent consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Min Jiang Kueh (Red Bean) is a traditional Singaporean street snack and breakfast item consisting of a thick, soft, leavened wheat-flour pancake filled with sweetened red bean paste. The pancake batter is made from wheat flour, eggs, sugar, baking powder, and a small amount of oil, then pan-fried until golden and slightly crispy on the outside while remaining fluffy and chewy inside. The red bean filling is made from adzuki beans cooked with sugar to form a smooth or semi-smooth paste. The snack is commonly sold by hawker stalls and pasar malam vendors across Singapore and is associated with Hokkien Chinese culinary tradition, though it has been widely adopted across ethnic communities. Per 100 g, it provides 206 kcal, 4.55 g protein, 2.91 g fat, 40.39 g carbohydrate, 12.59 g sugar, 2.6 g dietary fibre, and 212 mg sodium. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium) with a glycemic load of approximately 26.3 per 100 g serving, making it a high-glycemic-load food unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The refined flour base and moderate sugar content also present moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The food is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and eggs.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F11090037), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from a USDA SR Legacy analogous entry and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.7 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #18263 (pancakes, plain, prepared from recipe) as the HPB Foodo database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean snack preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on plain wheat-flour pancake (GI ~67, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted for red bean filling fibre contribution; no direct GI measurement for Min Jiang Kueh (Red Bean) exists in any approved source. 4. The input record described the cuisine as 'malay' but Min Jiang Kueh is of Hokkien Chinese origin and widely consumed across Singapore; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean / Peranakan Chinese'. 5. Description in input stated 'steamed thick pancake' but Min Jiang Kueh is pan-fried, not steamed; preparation method corrected accordingly.
